source_paper: Experts’ recommendations for the management of adult patients with cardiogenic shock
author: Nadia Aissaoui et al.
pearl: For patients with cardiogenic shock, utilize a multidisciplinary 'Shock Team' consisting of at least a heart failure specialist, an interventional cardiologist, and an intensivist or cardiac anesthesiologist to facilitate rapid SCAI staging and collaborative decision-making regarding temporary mechanical circulatory support (t-MCS).

source_paper: JCS 2026 Guideline on the Management of Infective Endocarditis
author: Chisato Izumi et al.
pearl: Staphylococcus aureus (especially MRSA) has surpassed viridans group streptococci as the most common and virulent pathogen in developed countries, causing rapid valvular destruction, perivalvular abscesses, and systemic embolization.

source_paper: JCS 2026 Guideline on the Management of Infective Endocarditis
author: Chisato Izumi et al.
pearl: Under the 2023 Duke-ISCVID criteria, a definitive clinical diagnosis of infective endocarditis requires the presence of either 2 major criteria, 1 major and 3 minor criteria, or 5 minor criteria.

source_paper: JCS 2026 Guideline on the Management of Infective Endocarditis
author: Chisato Izumi et al.
pearl: To satisfy the major microbiological criterion, typical pathogens (such as S. aureus, S. lugdunensis, E. faecalis, VGS, and HACEK) must be isolated from at least 2 separate blood culture sets, whereas less common pathogens require isolation from at least 3 separate sets.

source_paper: JCS 2026 Guideline on the Management of Infective Endocarditis
author: Chisato Izumi et al.
pearl: Major microbiological criteria for intracellular pathogens are met with a positive PCR for Coxiella burnetii, Bartonella spp., or Tropheryma whipplei, an IgG anti-phase I titer for C. burnetii > 1:800, or an IFA IgG titer >= 1:800 for B. henselae or B. quintana.

source_paper: JCS 2026 Guideline on the Management of Infective Endocarditis
author: Chisato Izumi et al.
pearl: When using 18F-FDG PET/CT to identify abnormal metabolic activity as a major imaging criterion, the prosthetic valve must have been implanted for at least 3 months to avoid false-positive results from physiological postoperative inflammation.

source_paper: JCS 2026 Guideline on the Management of Infective Endocarditis
author: Chisato Izumi et al.
pearl: Glomerulonephritis qualifies as an immunological minor criterion for infective endocarditis and is clinically defined by acute kidney injury associated with hematuria, proteinuria, red cell casts, or hypocomplementemia.

source_paper: Central Venous Pressure Revisited: Physiology, Pitfalls, Misconceptions, and Modern Clinical Interpretation in Critical Care

source_paper: Central Venous Pressure Revisited: Physiology, Pitfalls, Misconceptions, and Modern Clinical Interpretation in Critical Care
author: Cesare Biuzzi et al.
pearl: The historical goal-directed target for static CVP (8-12 mmHg or 12-15 mmHg in mechanically ventilated patients) has been abandoned by major guidelines due to lack of survival benefit in septic shock.

pearl: For HRS-AKI, co-administer albumin (20-40 g/day) with vasoconstrictors like terlipressin or norepinephrine.

author: Jonathan Soldera

source_paper: Beyond the Tipping Point: Advances in the Diagnosis and Management of Acute-on-Chronic Liver Failure and End-Stage Liver Disease
author: Jonathan Soldera

source_paper: Beyond the Tipping Point: Advances in the Diagnosis and Management of Acute-on-Chronic Liver Failure and End-Stage Liver Disease
author: Jonathan Soldera
pearl: In the management of severe ACLF, early enteral nutrition is highly recommended; avoid protein restriction even in HE patients.

source_paper: Beyond the Tipping Point: Advances in the Diagnosis and Management of Acute-on-Chronic Liver Failure and End-Stage Liver Disease
author: Jonathan Soldera

source_paper: Beyond the Tipping Point: Advances in the Diagnosis and Management of Acute-on-Chronic Liver Failure and End-Stage Liver Disease

source_paper: Gut microbiome-brain-cirrhosis axis
author: Maren L. Smith et al.
pearl: For recurrent overt HE, combine lactulose with rifaximin (550 mg BID) to target both ammonia generation and systemic neuroinflammation.

source_paper: Gut microbiome-brain-cirrhosis axis

source_paper: Gut microbiome-brain-cirrhosis axis

source_paper: Gut microbiome-brain-cirrhosis axis

source_paper: Gut microbiome-brain-cirrhosis axis

author: Maren L. Smith et al.

source_paper: Gut microbiome-brain-cirrhosis axis

author: Sanju Mathew et al.

source_paper: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC): An update on risk factors, surveillance, diagnosis and treatment strategies

author: Sanju Mathew et al.
pearl: For Intermediate Stage (BCLC B) HCC, locoregional therapies like TACE, bland embolisation (TAE), or SIRT are indicated, exploiting the liver's dual blood supply (80% portal vein, 20% hepatic artery).

source_paper: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC): An update on risk factors, surveillance, diagnosis and treatment strategies
author: Sanju Mathew et al.
pearl: For Advanced Stage (BCLC C) HCC with compensated function, Atezolizumab + Bevacizumab or the STRIDE regimen are first-line systemic therapies; Atezolizumab/Bevacizumab showed a median OS of 19.2 months (IMbrave150).

source_paper: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC): An update on risk factors, surveillance, diagnosis and treatment strategies
author: Sanju Mathew et al.

source_paper: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC): An update on risk factors, surveillance, diagnosis and treatment strategies
author: Sanju Mathew et al.
pearl: AFP is the only validated biomarker for surveillance but has a sensitivity of less than 50% when used alone and can be falsely elevated in pregnancy or active hepatitis.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: Perform diagnostic ascitic tap immediately for any patient with ascites, without waiting for coagulation correction.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: Administer parenteral thiamine (>= 72h if Wernicke's suspected; 24h if not) before glucose-containing fluids in alcohol-related liver disease.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: For SBP, initiate renal protection with intravenous 20% albumin (1.5 g/kg at diagnosis, 1 g/kg at 72 hours).

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: In AKI management, suspend all NSAIDs, diuretics, and beta-blockers; for HRS-AKI, consider Terlipressin (2-8 mg/day) plus 20% Albumin (100 mL BID).

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: For AVH, administer Terlipressin (2 mg IV bolus, then 1-2 mg QDS) and discontinue it 24 hours post-endoscopy.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: Maintain a restrictive transfusion strategy for AVH: transfuse PRBCs only if Hb < 70 g/dL (target 70-80 g/dL); avoid FFP/platelets to correct labs.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: A diagnostic paracentesis must be performed in all patients with ascites within the first 6 hours to rule out SBP, regardless of symptoms or inflammatory markers.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: SBP is diagnosed with ascitic fluid PMN count > 250/mm3.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: Secondary prophylaxis for SBP should start immediately after acute antibiotic course completion (e.g., co-trimoxazole 960 mg daily).

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: Initial AKI management requires suspending diuretics, beta-blockers, and NSAIDs; crystalloid boluses of 250 mL can be used to target UO > 0.5 mL/kg/hr.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: HDU escalation for AKI is needed if there is persistent hypotension, UO < 0.5 mL/kg/hr, rising lactate, or respiratory failure after 6 hours.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: For HRS-AKI, pharmacotherapy includes Terlipressin (2-8 mg daily, titrated) combined with 20% albumin (100 mL BID).

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: All upper GI bleeds in cirrhotic patients should be treated as AVH until proven otherwise.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: Pre-endoscopy pharmacotherapy for suspected AVH includes Terlipressin (2 mg IV bolus, then 1-2 mg QDS) or Octreotide (50 mcg IV bolus, then 50 mcg/hr infusion if terlipressin is contraindicated).

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: Empirical IV antibiotics and IV erythromycin (250 mg) are recommended pre-endoscopy for AVH.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: HE management requires titrating lactulose to achieve 2-3 soft stools daily; Rifaximin is reserved for secondary prevention of recurrent/chronic HE and does not acutely improve active episodes.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: For non-tense ascites or peripheral edema, diuretics are used (Spironolactone 100 mg daily + Furosemide 40 mg daily), titrated every 72 hours to target 0.5-1.0 kg daily weight loss.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: Malnutrition requires a high-protein diet (1.5 g/kg/day) with small, frequent meals and aggressive replacement of potassium, magnesium, and phosphate.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: VTE prophylaxis with tinzaparin is required for all patients with a platelet count > 50 x 10^9/L in the absence of active bleeding.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: Corticosteroid therapy (Prednisolone 40 mg daily) is indicated for severe alcohol-associated hepatitis (AH), determined by prognostic scores like Maddrey's Discriminant Function or MELD, after excluding active infection and GI bleeding.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: If a patient with severe AH is a non-responder (Lille score > 0.45) at Day 4-7, corticosteroid therapy should be stopped immediately.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: For urgent liver transplant assessment in ARLD, consider patients < 60 years old with 3-organ failure, no contraindications, and at least 3 months of documented abstinence.

pearl: Implement routine FIB-4 screening for high-risk populations (T2D, obesity, metabolic syndrome), integrating it into annual diabetes reviews.

source_paper: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease

author: Catherine Hsu et al.

source_paper: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease

source_paper: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease
author: Catherine Hsu et al.

source_paper: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease

source_paper: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease
author: Catherine Hsu et al.
pearl: Statins are safe and essential for primary/secondary prevention in MASLD patients due to the critical role of cardiovascular protection.

source_paper: Palliative care in advanced liver disease
author: Christopher Cussen et al.
pearl: Restrict paracetamol use to 2–3 g/24h in stable cirrhosis; for acute end-of-life pain, subcutaneous morphine 2.5 mg hourly is appropriate, but substitute with fentanyl if eGFR is reduced.

source_paper: Palliative care in advanced liver disease
author: Christopher Cussen et al.
pearl: For patients discharged after hepatic encephalopathy (HE), a clear lactulose titration plan targeting 2–3 soft stools/day and consideration of rifaximin are essential components of care.

author: Christopher Cussen et al.
pearl: Implement the Bristol Screening Tool on acute medical admission: a score > 2 suggests high risk of death within 12 months and mandates immediate supportive/palliative referral and Advance Care Planning discussion while cognitive capacity is retained.

source_paper: Palliative care in advanced liver disease
author: Christopher Cussen et al.

source_paper: Palliative care in advanced liver disease
author: Christopher Cussen et al.
pearl: Early palliative care referral in HCC is associated with fewer inpatient bed days, lower rates of in-hospital death, and reduced healthcare costs compared to standard care.

source_paper: Palliative care in advanced liver disease
author: Christopher Cussen et al.
pearl: Sarcopenia prevalence correlates strongly with frailty and poor clinical outcomes; early dietician involvement for nutritional optimization improves QOL and reduces covert HE symptoms.

source_paper: Palliative care in advanced liver disease

source_paper: Palliative care in advanced liver disease

source_paper: Palliative care in advanced liver disease
author: Christopher Cussen et al.
pearl: In patients with concurrent renal impairment or hepatorenal syndrome, morphine must be avoided or replaced with fentanyl when managing pain due to risk of toxic metabolite accumulation.

source_paper: Palliative care in advanced liver disease

source_paper: Palliative care in advanced liver disease
author: Christopher Cussen et al.

source_paper: Comparative efficacy and safety of immunomodulatory therapies for sepsis: a systematic review and network meta-analysis

source_paper: Comparative efficacy and safety of immunomodulatory therapies for sepsis: a systematic review and network meta-analysis
author: Luo R, et al.
pearl: Corticosteroids and G/M-CSF did not demonstrate a survival benefit in sepsis according to this network meta-analysis.

author: Luo R, et al.

source_paper: Remote ischemic conditioning in intensive care: From bench to bedside

source_paper: Remote ischemic conditioning in intensive care: From bench to bedside

source_paper: Remote ischemic conditioning in intensive care: From bench to bedside

source_paper: Remote ischemic conditioning in intensive care: From bench to bedside

source_paper: Remote ischemic conditioning in intensive care: From bench to bedside

source_paper: Remote ischemic conditioning in intensive care: From bench to bedside

pearl: Early identification of acute kidney injury (AKI) is crucial; use standardized scoring systems (e.g., KDIGO criteria) for staging and monitoring progression.

pearl: Do not withhold life-saving radiological imaging or emergency medications due to fears of fetal harm; delaying diagnosis causes greater maternal and fetal harm.

source_paper: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026
pearl: The use of prophylactic antibiotics should be avoided unless there is a clear indication or high risk of infection.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: Fidaxomicin 200 mg orally twice daily for 10 days is first‑line therapy for nonfulminant CDI because it lowers recurrence rates compared with vancomycin.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: Vancomycin taper/pulse regimen: 125 mg 4×/day for 14 days, then 125 mg 2×/day for 14 days, then 125 mg 1×/day for 14 days, then every third day for 14 days.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: Prior to FMT, complete a course of standard CDI antimicrobial treatment and maintain suppressive oral vancomycin ≥125 mg daily until therapy is available.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: Recurrent CDI risk exceeds 30% after discontinuation of vancomycin prophylaxis.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: Routine test of cure should not be performed after CDI treatment; stool testing is reserved for persistent or worsening diarrheal symptoms.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: Probiotics are not advised to prevent an initial or recurrent CDI.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: Use of vancomycin during systemic antibiotic administration to prevent CDI is not advised.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: PPIs may increase CDI risk, but the absolute risk is low; if no indication, discuss cessation.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: Patients with a history of CDI should be counseled to avoid unnecessary antibiotic therapy.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review

source_paper: Guidelines for the management of emergencies and critical illness in pediatric and adult patients with sickle cell disease

source_paper: Guidelines for the management of emergencies and critical illness in pediatric and adult patients with sickle cell disease

source_paper: Guidelines for the management of emergencies and critical illness in pediatric and adult patients with sickle cell disease

source_paper: Guidelines for the management of emergencies and critical illness in pediatric and adult patients with sickle cell disease

source_paper: Guidelines for the management of emergencies and critical illness in pediatric and adult patients with sickle cell disease

pearl: In starvation ketosis, plasma bicarbonate stabilizes at ~18 mEq/L and β‑hydroxybutyrate rises to 8–10 mmol/L, distinguishing it from diabetic ketoacidosis where regulatory feedback is absent.

source_paper: Pathophysiology of Ketoacidosis: Core Curriculum 2026
author: Biff F. Palmer and Deborah J. Clegg
pearl: During recovery from diabetic ketoacidosis, a hyperchloremic normal anion‑gap acidosis often redevelops due to indirect urinary bicarbonate loss.

source_paper: Pathophysiology of Ketoacidosis: Core Curriculum 2026
author: Biff F. Palmer and Deborah J. Clegg
pearl: Alcoholic ketoacidosis is characterized by a markedly elevated NADH/NAD⁺ ratio, driving β‑hydroxybutyrate production; direct measurement of β‑hydroxybutyrate is essential because urine strips detect only acetoacetate.

source_paper: Pathophysiology of Ketoacidosis: Core Curriculum 2026

pearl: Pregnancy‑associated euglycemic ketoacidosis shows a decrease in plasma bicarbonate of ~5 mEq/L for every 10 mm Hg drop in PaCO₂ due to chronic respiratory alkalosis.

source_paper: Pathophysiology of Ketoacidosis: Core Curriculum 2026

pearl: Lean body habitus and low‑carbohydrate intake are key risk factors for SGLT2‑inhibitor‑induced euglycemic ketoacidosis, as glycosuric response is independent of body size.

source_paper: Pathophysiology of Ketoacidosis: Core Curriculum 2026
author: Biff F. Palmer and Deborah J. Clegg

source_paper: Pathophysiology of Ketoacidosis: Core Curriculum 2026
author: Biff F. Palmer and Deborah J. Clegg

source_paper: Pathophysiology of Ketoacidosis: Core Curriculum 2026
author: Biff F. Palmer and Deborah J. Clegg

source_paper: Pathophysiology of Ketoacidosis: Core Curriculum 2026
author: Biff F. Palmer and Deborah J. Clegg
pearl: Chronic salicylate poisoning presents with a mixed respiratory alkalosis and high anion‑gap metabolic acidosis with ketosis; tinnitus and tachypnea are key clinical clues.

source_paper: Endovascular Thrombectomy in Medium and Distal Vessel Occlusions: A Focused Guideline From the Society of Vascular and Interventional Neurology Guidelines and Practice Standards Committee
pearl: Routine endovascular thrombectomy (EVT) for all medium and distal vessel occlusions (DMVO) is not supported by current randomized trial evidence; a tailored approach based on vascular territory, stroke severity, and tissue at risk is essential.

source_paper: Endovascular Thrombectomy in Medium and Distal Vessel Occlusions: A Focused Guideline From the Society of Vascular and Interventional Neurology Guidelines and Practice Standards Committee
pearl: EVT is reasonable (Class IIa) for disabling proximal or dominant M2 occlusions with moderate‑to‑severe deficits and imaging evidence of salvageable brain tissue, per HERMES and observational data.

source_paper: Endovascular Thrombectomy in Medium and Distal Vessel Occlusions: A Focused Guideline From the Society of Vascular and Interventional Neurology Guidelines and Practice Standards Committee
pearl: In codominant or nondominant M2 occlusions with NIHSS ≥ 6, EVT may be considered (Class IIb) based on ORIENTAL‑MeVO trial showing improved functional independence.

source_paper: Endovascular Thrombectomy in Medium and Distal Vessel Occlusions: A Focused Guideline From the Society of Vascular and Interventional Neurology Guidelines and Practice Standards Committee
pearl: Routine EVT is not recommended (Class III) for M3/M4, A2/A3, and P1‑P3 occlusions because ESCAPE‑MeVO, DISTAL, and DISCOUNT trials showed neutral or negative outcomes.

source_paper: Endovascular Thrombectomy in Medium and Distal Vessel Occlusions: A Focused Guideline From the Society of Vascular and Interventional Neurology Guidelines and Practice Standards Committee
pearl: The DISTALS trial demonstrated that the Tigertriever 13 adjustable stent retriever achieved a 3‑fold higher successful reperfusion rate without symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage (86.3% vs 27.7%, P<0.001) in codominant/nondominant M2 occlusions.

source_paper: Endovascular Thrombectomy in Medium and Distal Vessel Occlusions: A Focused Guideline From the Society of Vascular and Interventional Neurology Guidelines and Practice Standards Committee
pearl: Patient selection for DMVO EVT should prioritize clinical severity (NIHSS ≥ 6 or disabling deficit), limited infarct core, favorable collaterals, and rapid treatment eligibility, and should avoid EVT in low‑severity, nondisabling strokes.

source_paper: Endovascular Thrombectomy in Medium and Distal Vessel Occlusions: A Focused Guideline From the Society of Vascular and Interventional Neurology Guidelines and Practice Standards Committee
pearl: Subgroup analyses suggest that EVT outcomes improve when reperfusion is achieved within 4.5 hours from symptom onset.

source_paper: Endovascular Thrombectomy in Medium and Distal Vessel Occlusions: A Focused Guideline From the Society of Vascular and Interventional Neurology Guidelines and Practice Standards Committee
pearl: Aspiration‑first approach is comparable to stent retriever in M2 occlusions, but aspiration may be preferable in tortuous distal vessels to reduce hemorrhagic complications.

source_paper: Endovascular Thrombectomy in Medium and Distal Vessel Occlusions: A Focused Guideline From the Society of Vascular and Interventional Neurology Guidelines and Practice Standards Committee
pearl: Vessel perforation incidence in DMVO is 4.8% overall, higher in M3 (8.9%) and A2 (8.3%) compared with M2 (4.3%).

source_paper: Endovascular Thrombectomy in Medium and Distal Vessel Occlusions: A Focused Guideline From the Society of Vascular and Interventional Neurology Guidelines and Practice Standards Committee
pearl: Symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage rates were 5.4% with EVT versus 2.2% with medical therapy in ESCAPE‑MeVO, and 12% versus 6% in DISCOUNT.

source_paper: Endovascular Thrombectomy in Medium and Distal Vessel Occlusions: A Focused Guideline From the Society of Vascular and Interventional Neurology Guidelines and Practice Standards Committee
pearl: Mortality was higher in the EVT arm of ESCAPE‑MeVO (13.3% vs 8.4%, adjusted HR 1.82) but similar between EVT and medical arms in DISTAL (15.5% vs 14%).

source_paper: Endovascular Thrombectomy in Medium and Distal Vessel Occlusions: A Focused Guideline From the Society of Vascular and Interventional Neurology Guidelines and Practice Standards Committee
pearl: For M3/M4 occlusions, consider EVT only in patients with NIHSS ≥ 5, eloquent deficits (aphasia, hemianopia, hemiparesis), large infarct burden, and favorable procedural risk.

source_paper: Endovascular Thrombectomy in Medium and Distal Vessel Occlusions: A Focused Guideline From the Society of Vascular and Interventional Neurology Guidelines and Practice Standards Committee
pearl: Routine EVT is not recommended for isolated ACA (A2/A3) occlusions due to insufficient evidence of benefit.

source_paper: Endovascular Thrombectomy in Medium and Distal Vessel Occlusions: A Focused Guideline From the Society of Vascular and Interventional Neurology Guidelines and Practice Standards Committee
pearl: In ACA occlusions with disabling deficits, large territory at risk, favorable imaging, and good baseline function, EVT may be considered on a case‑by‑case basis.

source_paper: Endovascular Thrombectomy in Medium and Distal Vessel Occlusions: A Focused Guideline From the Society of Vascular and Interventional Neurology Guidelines and Practice Standards Committee
pearl: Routine EVT is not recommended for isolated PCA (P1‑P3) occlusions, but may be considered in selected patients with early presentation, severe deficits impacting quality of life, or IV thrombolysis ineligibility.

source_paper: Endovascular Thrombectomy in Medium and Distal Vessel Occlusions: A Focused Guideline From the Society of Vascular and Interventional Neurology Guidelines and Practice Standards Committee
pearl: For DMVO candidates, administer IV tPA if eligible and prioritize rapid EVT (<4.5 hours from onset) to maximize benefit.

source_paper: Endovascular Thrombectomy in Medium and Distal Vessel Occlusions: A Focused Guideline From the Society of Vascular and Interventional Neurology Guidelines and Practice Standards Committee
pearl: Use CT/CTP or MRI to confirm limited infarct core and favorable collateral status before proceeding with EVT.

source_paper: Endovascular Thrombectomy in Medium and Distal Vessel Occlusions: A Focused Guideline From the Society of Vascular and Interventional Neurology Guidelines and Practice Standards Committee
pearl: In M2 occlusions, an aspiration‑first strategy may be favorable, and purpose‑built adjustable stentrievers such as Tigertriever 13 should be considered for distal, tortuous vessels to minimize hemorrhagic complications.

source_paper: Endovascular Thrombectomy in Medium and Distal Vessel Occlusions: A Focused Guideline From the Society of Vascular and Interventional Neurology Guidelines and Practice Standards Committee
pearl: Careful guidewire and microcatheter selection is essential to reduce perforation risk in DMVO procedures.

source_paper: Endovascular Thrombectomy in Medium and Distal Vessel Occlusions: A Focused Guideline From the Society of Vascular and Interventional Neurology Guidelines and Practice Standards Committee
pearl: For M3/M4, A2/A3, and P1‑P3 occlusions, EVT is not routine; consider only in cases with clearly disabling deficits, large territory at risk, favorable anatomy, and enroll eligible patients in ongoing DMVO trials.

source_paper: Consensus statement from the 2025 Delphi panel on cerebral microdialysis in critical care
pearl: CMD monitoring should be considered for traumatic brain injury or subarachnoid hemorrhage when there is a concurrent indication for invasive cerebral monitoring or neurosurgical access.

source_paper: Consensus statement from the 2025 Delphi panel on cerebral microdialysis in critical care
pearl: CMD monitoring should be considered for intracerebral hemorrhage in selected severe cases when there is a concurrent indication for invasive cerebral monitoring or neurosurgical access.

source_paper: Consensus statement from the 2025 Delphi panel on cerebral microdialysis in critical care
pearl: CMD catheter placement should be performed by a trained clinician (neurosurgeon, trainee, or intensivist) with overall responsibility held by a consultant neurosurgeon.

source_paper: Consensus statement from the 2025 Delphi panel on cerebral microdialysis in critical care
pearl: The first hour of microdialysate should be discarded because catheter insertion and pump flushing confound the data.

source_paper: Consensus statement from the 2025 Delphi panel on cerebral microdialysis in critical care
pearl: An elevated lactate/pyruvate ratio >25 with brain glucose <1.0 mM or pyruvate <70 µM indicates ischemia; with sufficient substrate (glucose ≥1.0 mM and pyruvate ≥70 µM) it indicates mitochondrial dysfunction.

source_paper: Consensus statement from the 2025 Delphi panel on cerebral microdialysis in critical care
pearl: When treating deranged metabolism, prioritize ICP ≤20 mmHg, then PbtO₂ ≥15 mmHg, then brain glucose; consider CPP optimization for refractory LPR >25.

source_paper: Consensus statement from the 2025 Delphi panel on cerebral microdialysis in critical care
pearl: Hourly CMD sampling provides clinical benefit; higher‑frequency or continuous monitoring may increase utility as online systems develop.

source_paper: Consensus statement from the 2025 Delphi panel on cerebral microdialysis in critical care
pearl: Maintain safe serum glucose below 10 mM (180 mg/dL) even when treating neuroglycopenia.

source_paper: Consensus statement from the 2025 Delphi panel on cerebral microdialysis in critical care
pearl: Intravenous dextrose may be titrated to raise plasma glucose up to 10 mM (180 mg/dL) to resolve neuroglycopenia.

source_paper: Consensus statement from the 2025 Delphi panel on cerebral microdialysis in critical care
pearl: An LPR >25 correlates with worse outcome; a higher threshold of 40 does not show a stronger association.

source_paper: Consensus statement from the 2025 Delphi panel on cerebral microdialysis in critical care
pearl: Elevated cerebral glycerol is an indicator of cell death and damage from secondary injury after traumatic brain injury.

source_paper: Consensus statement from the 2025 Delphi panel on cerebral microdialysis in critical care

pearl: Do not use IV prophylactic antibiotics in patients with short‑ or long‑term catheters.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part 2: central venous catheters
pearl: Use CVC practice bundles.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part 2: central venous catheters
pearl: Perform HAI surveillance and timely feedback to staff.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part 2: central venous catheters
pearl: Implement multimodal improvement strategy (MMIS) for IPC interventions.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part 2: central venous catheters
pearl: Use chlorhexidine‑containing body wash daily in critically ill adults, adolescents, and children.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part 2: central venous catheters
pearl: Use a standardized insertion pack/kit when placing a CVC.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part 2: central venous catheters
pearl: Use sterile single‑use gloves for CVC insertion.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part 2: central venous catheters
pearl: Use maximal sterile barrier precautions during CVC insertion.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part 2: central venous catheters
pearl: Use alcohol‑chlorhexidine 1–2% over other antiseptics for skin disinfection before CVC insertion.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part 2: central venous catheters
pearl: Use a transparent semi‑permeable dressing or gauze dressing after CVC insertion.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part 2: central venous catheters
pearl: Use an antiseptic patch or dressing (e.g., chlorhexidine‑gluconate‑impregnated sponge) at the CVC insertion site.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part 2: central venous catheters
pearl: Use a sterile dressing protocol at the insertion site.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part 2: central venous catheters
pearl: Use a formal schedule of dressing changes, with intervals of 3 days or more.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part 2: central venous catheters
pearl: Use either continuous or intermittent infusion for CVC maintenance.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part 2: central venous catheters
pearl: Flush CVC with a compatible sterile fluid after product administration.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Use an aseptic "no‑touch" technique for PIVC insertion; use a sterile technique (mask, gown, gloves, drape) for PICC and PAC insertion.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Apply 2% chlorhexidine gluconate in 70% alcohol for skin disinfection; ensure chlorhexidine concentration >0.5%, and avoid >2% in neonates <36 weeks.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: All clinicians inserting peripheral catheters must complete a formal training programme with demonstrated competency; this is essential for PICC/PAC and for all catheters in neonates.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Inspect PIVCs at least daily for inflammation or infection; consider removal within 72–96 h or when clinically indicated.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Prefer single‑lumen PICCs over multi‑lumen PICCs unless multiple lumens are clinically required, to reduce infection and thrombosis risk.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Perform hand hygiene at all five moments during catheter insertion, maintenance, access, and removal, using WHO alcohol‑based hand rub or soap and water.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Wear single‑use gloves when inserting a PIVC or PICC in adults, adolescents, or children; use sterile gloves for PICC/PAC; do not use gloves for PIVC if aseptic "no‑touch" technique is strictly followed.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Use a standardized insertion pack or kit for PICC or PAC insertion; for PIVC use a standardized pack/kit as well.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Use ultrasound guidance when inserting a PICC; routine ultrasound is not recommended for PIVC insertion.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Select distal arm veins over proximal (cubital fossa) for PIVC insertion; choose upper limb over lower limb for PIVC and PICC in neonates; avoid scalp veins in neonates.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Apply either an occlusive or non‑occlusive dressing for PIVCs; use an occlusive dressing for PICCs.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Maintain all PIVCs, PICCs, and PACs with a formal sterile dressing protocol.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Flush the catheter with a compatible sterile fluid (saline or other) after each product administration.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Prefer sterile saline for lock‑off flushing over heparinised saline to avoid heparin‑induced thrombocytopenia.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Change administration sets (tubing) on a regular schedule for PIVCs and PICCs.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Access catheters using a formal sterile or aseptic protocol.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Use closed‑access hub systems for PIVCs and PICCs to reduce CRBSI in PICCs and sepsis in neonates.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: If a PIVC is inserted under uncontrolled or emergency conditions, remove or replace it as soon as possible, ideally within 24 h.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Scheduled removal of PIVCs can be performed at 72–96 h; clinically indicated removal is also acceptable.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Use a multi‑lumen catheter for IV access only if the expected duration is less than 14 days; otherwise, choose a PICC.

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Give the first empiric antibiotic dose within 1 hour of presentation; do not delay for lumbar puncture or imaging.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: The classic triad of fever (≥38 °C), neck stiffness, and altered mental status is present in <50 % of bacterial meningitis cases; absence of all three lowers the likelihood of a bacterial cause.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Obtain CSF and blood for culture and PCR as soon as possible, ideally before starting antimicrobials, to maximize pathogen detection.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Empiric therapy for adults and children >1 month: IV ceftriaxone 2 g q12 h (or cefotaxime); add ampicillin or amoxicillin if Listeria risk factors (age > 60, pregnancy, immunocompromised).
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Adjunctive IV dexamethasone 10 mg q6 h (0.15 mg/kg in children) should start with the first antibiotic dose when bacterial meningitis is strongly suspected; discontinue if any pathogen other than S. pneumoniae or H. influenzae b is identified.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: In HIV‑associated cryptococcal meningitis, therapeutic lumbar puncture to reduce CSF opening pressure to <20 cm H₂O improves clinical outcome and survival, regardless of initial pressure.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: All patients who survive acute meningitis should have a complete neurological exam and formal audiological screening for hearing loss before discharge.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Use the Interagency Integrated Triage Tool (IITT) to identify high‑risk patients with abnormal vital signs for immediate review.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Absolute contraindications to lumbar puncture include ongoing hemodynamic instability, respiratory failure, severe bleeding disorder, skin infection over the puncture site, or epidural abscess near the site.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Relative contraindications requiring cranial imaging before lumbar puncture are GCS < 10, focal neurological signs, cranial nerve deficits, papilledema, new‑onset seizures in adults, or severe immunocompromise.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Collect at least three CSF tubes (≥1 mL each) for cellularity, biochemistry, Gram stain, culture, and PCR; if only one tube is available, prioritize microbiology.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Core CSF investigations: glucose, total protein, WBC count (total and differential), RBC count, lactate (if antibiotics not yet started), Gram stain, culture, AST, and PCR for bacteria and viruses.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: For suspected TB meningitis, use LC‑aNAAT (e.g., Xpert MTB/RIF Ultra); for suspected cryptococcal meningitis in advanced HIV, perform rapid CSF CrAg assay (or India ink if CrAg unavailable).
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Cloudy CSF with neutrophil predominance, low glucose, and high protein suggests bacterial meningitis; clear CSF with lymphocyte predominance and normal/moderately altered glucose and protein suggests viral meningitis.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: In malaria‑endemic areas, perform a parasitological test; if positive, treat for severe malaria but still perform lumbar puncture to rule out bacterial meningitis.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Empiric therapy for neonates: ampicillin plus gentamicin, or cefotaxime plus gentamicin, or ceftriaxone plus gentamicin.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Adjunctive dexamethasone (10 mg q6 h, 0.15 mg/kg in children) should be started with the first antibiotic dose within 12 h; continue up to 4 days if S. pneumoniae or H. influenzae b is identified; discontinue if other pathogens are found; do not give if cerebral malaria or cryptococcal meningitis is suspected.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Definitive antibiotic durations: S. pneumoniae 10–14 days; N. meningitidis 5–7 days; H. influenzae b 7–10 days; S. agalactiae 14–21 days; L. monocytogenes 21 days; Gram‑negative bacilli 14–21 days.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: If the pathogen remains unidentified, discontinue empiric antibiotics after 7 days if the patient is afebrile, vitals stable, and consciousness normal for ≥48 hours.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: TB meningitis treatment: adults – intensive 2 months HRZE, continuation 7–10 months HR (total 9–12 months); children – standard 12 months (2HRZE/10HR) or short 6‑month intensive regimen (6HRZEto) if no DR‑TB risk factors or HIV.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Cryptococcal meningitis induction: liposomal amphotericin B IV single high dose 10 mg/kg (or 3–4 mg/kg/day) plus flucytosine PO 25 mg/kg q6h and fluconazole PO/IV 1200 mg/day for 14 days, followed by consolidation and maintenance fluconazole.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Increased intracranial pressure ≥20 cm H₂O in adults is life‑threatening; signs include severe headache, vomiting, altered consciousness, and Cushing reflex.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Basic emergency measures for raised ICP: secure airway, avoid hypotension, maintain SpO₂ > 94 %, control fever <37.5 °C, elevate head 15–30°, consider hyperventilation if ventilated (target PaCO₂ 26–30 mmHg).
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: In cryptococcal meningitis, therapeutic lumbar puncture to reduce CSF pressure to <20 cm H₂O (or halve baseline) is recommended; mannitol, acetazolamide, furosemide, and corticosteroids are not recommended.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Seizures occur in 20–30 % of meningitis patients; acute symptomatic seizures are treated with IV benzodiazepines followed by second‑line agents if seizures persist.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: Critical care is a treatment, not a location; intensivists should support care outside the ICU.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: Pregnant patients are unfasted and at high aspiration risk; use rapid sequence induction with cricoid pressure and video laryngoscopy as first choice.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: Maintain maternal SpO2 >95% and PaO2 >9.3 kPa (70 mmHg) to ensure adequate fetal oxygenation.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: Noradrenaline is the preferred first‑line vasopressor in obstetric shock; vasopressin should be avoided.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: Initiate renal replacement therapy when serum urea reaches 17–20 mmol/L in pregnancy.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: During major obstetric haemorrhage resuscitation, keep fibrinogen >2 g/L using fibrinogen concentrate or cryoprecipitate.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: In pre‑eclampsia, give high‑dose remifentanil during intubation to blunt the hypertensive response and restrict fluids to 80 mL hr⁻¹.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: Prone positioning is safe for pregnant patients with severe hypoxaemia when continuous fetal monitoring is used.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: In shock, rule out inferior vena cava compression and use a 15–30° lateral tilt or left lateral decubitus position.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: Caution with fluid boluses; 30 mL kg⁻¹ may cause significant oedema in pregnancy.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: Ephedrine worsens fetal acidaemia in a dose‑dependent manner.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: Remifentanil is the first‑line opioid for sedation in obstetric ICU due to reliable neonatal metabolism.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: Avoid NSAIDs after 28 weeks gestation because of risk of premature ductus arteriosus closure.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: Target a blood glucose level of 6–8 mmol L⁻¹ in critically ill pregnant patients.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: Iodinated contrast is safe in pregnancy; gadolinium contrast should be avoided.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: Tranexamic acid is recommended for all cases of postpartum haemorrhage.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: Early delivery is recommended for obstetric syndromes such as pre‑eclampsia and HELLP; timing for non‑obstetric critical illness should be individualized by an MDT.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: Risk of major bleeding with thrombolysis for pulmonary embolism is higher postpartum (58 %) than antepartum (18 %).

source_paper: Current concepts on feeding the critically ill patient: a narrative review
author: Michael Adolph et al.
pearl: Patients with CRP >100 mg/dL or IL‑6 >11.17 pg/mL may not gain the same survival benefit from full nutritional therapy and require a more individualized approach.

source_paper: Current concepts on feeding the critically ill patient: a narrative review
author: Michael Adolph et al.
pearl: Set energy targets using indirect calorimetry and aim to reach them by day 5–7; predictive equations are unreliable in the acute phase.

source_paper: Current concepts on feeding the critically ill patient: a narrative review
author: Michael Adolph et al.
pearl: Avoid protein >1.2 g/kg/day early in ICU; target 1.0–1.2 g/kg/day and increase gradually over the first 4–5 days.

source_paper: Current concepts on feeding the critically ill patient: a narrative review
author: Michael Adolph et al.
pearl: A high urea‑to‑creatinine ratio identifies a metabolically distinct ICU subgroup with increased protein catabolism and may predict reduced benefit from high protein intake.

source_paper: Current concepts on feeding the critically ill patient: a narrative review
author: Michael Adolph et al.
pearl: Give thiamine 100 mg/day from admission and standard parenteral nutrition micronutrient doses for ~5 days; no blood monitoring is needed.

source_paper: Current concepts on feeding the critically ill patient: a narrative review
author: Michael Adolph et al.
pearl: A 4:1 ketogenic diet with β‑hydroxybutyrate 1–3 mmol/L is feasible in refractory status epilepticus and sepsis, reducing vasopressor use and improving glucose control.

source_paper: Current concepts on feeding the critically ill patient: a narrative review
author: Michael Adolph et al.
pearl: Post‑ICU survivors typically meet only 50–70 % of energy and protein needs; structured multidisciplinary rehabilitation and transitional nutrition programs are essential.

source_paper: Current concepts on feeding the critically ill patient: a narrative review
author: Michael Adolph et al.
pearl: Interpret micronutrient blood levels together with CRP because inflammation redistributes micronutrients.

source_paper: Current concepts on feeding the critically ill patient: a narrative review
author: Michael Adolph et al.
pearl: Treat macronutrients as pharmacotherapy; personalize to metabolic tolerance and organ function.

source_paper: Current concepts on feeding the critically ill patient: a narrative review
author: Michael Adolph et al.
pearl: Order nutrition as a rate (1 ml/kg/h ≈ 24 kcal/kg/day) to simplify monitoring and adjustment.

source_paper: Current concepts on feeding the critically ill patient: a narrative review
author: Michael Adolph et al.
pearl: Early initiation of a 4:1 ketogenic diet with BHB 1–3 mmol/L improves seizure resolution in adult super‑refractory status epilepticus.

source_paper: Current concepts on feeding the critically ill patient: a narrative review
author: Michael Adolph et al.
pearl: Ketogenic nutrition in sepsis improves glucose homeostasis, eliminates insulin need, and increases ventilator‑free, vasopressor‑free, dialysis‑free, and ICU‑free days.

source_paper: Current concepts on feeding the critically ill patient: a narrative review
author: Michael Adolph et al.
pearl: Repeat BIA and handgrip strength every 4–6 weeks for the first 6 months, then every 3 months, in post‑ICU patients.

source_paper: Current concepts on feeding the critically ill patient: a narrative review
author: Michael Adolph et al.
pearl: The EFFORT II trial evaluates a multidisciplinary outpatient nutrition program to improve long‑term survival and functional recovery after ICU discharge.

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: An oxygen extraction ratio (O2ER) >30% indicates that oxygen delivery (DO₂) is insufficient to meet metabolic demands.
topic: Oxygen extraction ratio, septic shock, resuscitation

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: An O2ER <20% may reflect impaired cellular oxygen utilization, shunting, or excessive oxygen delivery.
topic: Oxygen extraction ratio, septic shock, pathophysiology

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: Normal O2ER values lie between 20% and 30% and represent adequate balance of supply and demand.

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: Targeting an O2ER of 20–30% during resuscitation can guide fluid therapy and avoid under‑ or over‑resuscitation.

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: An O2ER >40% signals inadequate DO₂ and should prompt optimization of preload, hemoglobin, cardiac output, and arterial oxygenation.

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: An O2ER <20% combined with elevated ScvO₂ and rising lactate suggests impaired extraction; further increasing DO₂ alone is unlikely to help.

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: Changes in central venous oxygen saturation (ΔScvO₂) after fluid challenge identify fluid responders and correlate with cardiac index changes.

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: Lactate‑clearance‑guided resuscitation reduces in‑hospital mortality, shortens mechanical ventilation, and lowers severity scores compared with ScvO₂‑guided protocols.

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: Dynamic lactate measurements and lactate‑clearance trajectories predict 28‑day mortality better than static lactate levels.

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: Combining lactate and ScvO₂ levels stratifies mortality more effectively; elevated lactate with normal ScvO₂ is a strong predictor of poor outcome.

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: The ΔPv‑aCO₂/CaO₂‑CvO₂ ratio can detect early perfusion deficits and monitor metabolic shifts during resuscitation.
topic: CO₂ gap, perfusion monitoring, septic shock

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: ScvO₂ normalization alone is no longer a sufficient universal therapeutic target in contemporary septic shock phenotypes.

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: The Surviving Sepsis Campaign removed ScvO₂ ≥70% as a mandated target after neutral trials showed no mortality benefit.

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: ScvO₂ alone is insufficient to characterize circulatory status; multimodal integration with metabolic and perfusion indices is required.

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: Dynamic changes in O2ER often precede overt deterioration in vital signs, enabling earlier detection of occult hypoperfusion.
topic: Oxygen extraction ratio, early detection, septic shock

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: O2ER accuracy depends on reliable measurement of arterial and venous oxygen content and can be distorted by supraphysiologic DO₂ or microcirculatory shunting.
topic: Oxygen extraction ratio, measurement accuracy, septic shock

source_paper: Sepsis-associated sarcopenia: Emerging insights into mechanisms and therapeutic approaches
author: Xiujun Chang et al.
pearl: Sarcopenia affects 40–70% of sepsis survivors and can persist up to 2 years after ICU discharge, significantly impacting long‑term quality of life and mortality.

source_paper: Sepsis-associated sarcopenia: Emerging insights into mechanisms and therapeutic approaches
author: Xiujun Chang et al.
pearl: A 1% reduction in rectus femoris cross‑sectional area on bedside ultrasound is associated with a 6% increase in 28‑day mortality, making it an independent prognostic factor.

source_paper: Sepsis-associated sarcopenia: Emerging insights into mechanisms and therapeutic approaches
author: Xiujun Chang et al.
pearl: Diagnosis of sepsis‑associated sarcopenia relies on non‑invasive measures such as handgrip strength, MRC score, bedside ultrasound of rectus femoris or diaphragm, and CT‑derived muscle indices (PMI, SMI); muscle biopsy is not routinely recommended.

source_paper: Sepsis-associated sarcopenia: Emerging insights into mechanisms and therapeutic approaches
author: Xiujun Chang et al.
pearl: Initiating enteral nutrition within 48 hours of hemodynamic stabilization is associated with lower in‑hospital mortality in patients with pre‑existing sarcopenia (OCEBM Level 2b).

source_paper: Sepsis-associated sarcopenia: Emerging insights into mechanisms and therapeutic approaches
author: Xiujun Chang et al.
pearl: Early mobilization within the first 3 days of ICU admission significantly improves outcomes (OCEBM Level 2b).

source_paper: Sepsis-associated sarcopenia: Emerging insights into mechanisms and therapeutic approaches
author: Xiujun Chang et al.
pearl: Ultrasound assessment of diaphragm and intercostal muscle echogenicity and thickening fraction may help predict weaning outcomes.

source_paper: Sepsis-associated sarcopenia: Emerging insights into mechanisms and therapeutic approaches
author: Xiujun Chang et al.
pearl: Reduced psoas muscle index on CT independently correlates with 28‑ and 90‑day mortality and longer ICU/hospital stay.

source_paper: Sepsis-associated sarcopenia: Emerging insights into mechanisms and therapeutic approaches

source_paper: Sepsis-associated sarcopenia: Emerging insights into mechanisms and therapeutic approaches

source_paper: Sepsis-associated sarcopenia: Emerging insights into mechanisms and therapeutic approaches

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: Fever of unknown origin is defined as a temperature ≥38.3 °C (101 °F) lasting >3 weeks that remains undiagnosed after a standard work‑up, and it is now often evaluated as an outpatient.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: The diagnostic work‑up for FUO should be clue‑driven, first categorizing the case into malignant/neoplastic, infectious, rheumatic/inflammatory, or miscellaneous disorders based on focused history and physical examination.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: Early anorexia with significant weight loss (>2 lb/week) strongly suggests a malignant or neoplastic etiology.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: Chills are common in infectious FUOs, whereas rigors essentially rule out rheumatic or inflammatory causes.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: An erythrocyte sedimentation rate >100 mm/h markedly narrows the differential to a few entities.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: The proportion of atypical lymphocytes matters: 6 % atypical lymphocytes point to drug fever or toxoplasmosis, while 36 % atypical lymphocytes suggest EBV or CMV.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: The Naprosyn test (375 mg naproxen twice daily for 3 days) is positive when the temperature falls markedly over 3 days, indicating a malignant or neoplastic FUO; a negative test (persistent fever) suggests an infectious etiology.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: Posterior cervical, supra‑/infraclavicular, or epitrochlear lymph node biopsies have higher diagnostic yield than anterior cervical, axillary, or inguinal node biopsies in FUO.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: In HIV‑associated FUO, drug fever occurs in 3–20 % of cases, and immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) typically develops within 60 days of initiating HAART.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: Routine blood cultures are unnecessary for non‑bacteremic FUOs and should be avoided early in the work‑up.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: An isolated alkaline phosphatase elevation is suggestive of lymphoma in the FUO work‑up.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: Microscopic hematuria may be the sole clue to subacute bacterial endocarditis, renal tuberculosis, brucellosis, PAN, lymphoma, or renal cell carcinoma.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: PET‑CT is most useful for obscure infectious or neoplastic FUOs, such as lymphomas, Erdheim‑Chester disease, Q fever endocarditis, or aortic graft infection.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: Empiric therapy should be reserved for life‑threatening FUOs such as CNS or miliary tuberculosis or giant cell arteritis/temporal arteritis.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: Recurrent FUO is defined as at least two episodes of prolonged fever separated by ≥2 weeks of fever‑free intervals; if it remains undiagnosed for >1 year, a definitive diagnosis is unlikely.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: Antipyretic or antimicrobial therapy may mask, delay, or obscure clinical manifestations in FUO and should be avoided until a diagnosis is established.
