source_paper: Practical Guide: Usual-care mobilisation of adult patients in the intensive care unit
author: Paton M, Hodgson CL
pearl: Implementation barriers—staffing, competing priorities, equipment, sedation practices, clinician concerns—can be addressed through clear local protocols, conservative and individualized dosing, alignment of sedation and mobilisation goals, multidisciplinary communication, and routine review of mobilisation practice and adverse event data.
file_name: Usual-care mobilisation of adult patients inthe intensive care unit.json

source_paper: Practical Guide: Usual-care mobilisation of adult patients in the intensive care unit
author: Paton M, Hodgson CL
pearl: A traffic‑light screening checklist enables consistent identification of physiological and procedural risk while avoiding unnecessary exclusion from mobilisation.
file_name: Usual-care mobilisation of adult patients inthe intensive care unit.json

source_paper: Practical Guide: Usual-care mobilisation of adult patients in the intensive care unit
author: Paton M, Hodgson CL
pearl: Markers of excessive intensity—sustained tachyarrhythmia, marked desaturation, or hypotension—prompt immediate dose reduction or cessation of mobilisation.
file_name: Usual-care mobilisation of adult patients inthe intensive care unit.json
topic: Intensity adverse events, tachyarrhythmia, desaturation, hypotension

source_paper: Practical Guide: Usual-care mobilisation of adult patients in the intensive care unit
author: Paton M, Hodgson CL
pearl: Progression should be adaptive within and between sessions based on tolerance; regression is a legitimate and appropriate response to day‑to‑day clinical fluctuation.
file_name: Usual-care mobilisation of adult patients inthe intensive care unit.json

source_paper: Practical Guide: Usual-care mobilisation of adult patients in the intensive care unit
author: Paton M, Hodgson CL
pearl: Selection of mobility level should prioritize the lowest level that achieves therapeutic intent, rather than default progression to upright or ambulatory activity.
file_name: Usual-care mobilisation of adult patients inthe intensive care unit.json

source_paper: Pulmonary aspergillosis: diagnosis and treatment
author: Lamoth F, Calandra T
pearl: Combined serum galactomannan and Aspergillus PCR in haematologic cancer patients yields 99% sensitivity and 98% specificity for invasive pulmonary aspergillosis diagnosis.

source_paper: Pulmonary aspergillosis: diagnosis and treatment
author: Lamoth F, Calandra T
pearl: In solid organ transplant recipients, serum galactomannan sensitivity is only ~40%, but bronchoalveolar lavage galactomannan and PCR maintain high diagnostic accuracy.

source_paper: Pulmonary aspergillosis: diagnosis and treatment
author: Lamoth F, Calandra T
pearl: Voriconazole therapeutic drug monitoring target trough concentration is 1–5.5 mg/L.

source_paper: Pulmonary aspergillosis: diagnosis and treatment
author: Lamoth F, Calandra T
pearl: Posaconazole therapeutic drug monitoring target trough concentration is >0.7 mg/L for prophylaxis and >1.0 mg/L for treatment.

source_paper: Pulmonary aspergillosis: diagnosis and treatment
author: Lamoth F, Calandra T
pearl: Isavuconazole is associated with less than 10% hepatotoxicity, fewer drug interactions, and does not require routine therapeutic drug monitoring.

source_paper: Pulmonary aspergillosis: diagnosis and treatment
author: Lamoth F, Calandra T
pearl: Liposomal amphotericin B is second‑line for proven or probable IPA, first‑line for azole‑resistant IPA, and is recommended as initial empirical therapy for possible invasive mould infection.

source_paper: Pulmonary aspergillosis: diagnosis and treatment
author: Lamoth F, Calandra T
pearl: Antifungal therapy for suspected IPA should not be delayed by more than 24 hours after bronchoscopy.

source_paper: Pulmonary aspergillosis: diagnosis and treatment
author: Lamoth F, Calandra T
pearl: Chronic pulmonary aspergillosis patients on long‑term triazole therapy should undergo dermatologic screening every 6 months to detect early skin cancer.

source_paper: Pulmonary aspergillosis: diagnosis and treatment
author: Lamoth F, Calandra T
pearl: In chronic pulmonary aspergillosis, anti‑Aspergillus IgG or precipitins have 75–80% sensitivity for aspergilloma and are recommended for diagnosis and monitoring.

source_paper: Pulmonary aspergillosis: diagnosis and treatment
author: Lamoth F, Calandra T
pearl: In chronic pulmonary aspergillosis, bronchoalveolar lavage galactomannan shows 75–85% sensitivity and 75–80% specificity; serum galactomannan is not recommended.

source_paper: Pulmonary aspergillosis: diagnosis and treatment
author: Lamoth F, Calandra T
pearl: Chronic pulmonary aspergillosis management includes routine chest CT scans every 3 or 6 months to assess treatment response.

source_paper: Pulmonary aspergillosis: diagnosis and treatment
author: Lamoth F, Calandra T
pearl: Long‑term suppressive oral triazole therapy is the cornerstone of chronic pulmonary aspergillosis treatment; itraconazole is first‑line, voriconazole or posaconazole for more severe disease, and isavuconazole for toxicity or drug‑interaction concerns.

source_paper: Pulmonary aspergillosis: diagnosis and treatment
author: Lamoth F, Calandra T
pearl: Surgical indications in chronic pulmonary aspergillosis are individualized; pre‑operative antifungal therapy may be needed for complex procedures, and post‑operative therapy is considered after partial resection, risk of spillage, or histopathologic evidence of parenchymal invasion.

source_paper: Pulmonary aspergillosis: diagnosis and treatment
author: Lamoth F, Calandra T
pearl: For chronic pulmonary aspergillosis patients who cannot undergo surgery and have recurrent haemoptysis, direct intracavitary instillation of amphotericin B or a triazole has shown some efficacy.

source_paper: Pulmonary aspergillosis: diagnosis and treatment
author: Lamoth F, Calandra T
pearl: Haemoptysis in chronic pulmonary aspergillosis is managed with tranexamic acid or arterial embolisation for severe cases, and surgery for severe or recurrent haemoptysis.

source_paper: Pulmonary aspergillosis: diagnosis and treatment
author: Lamoth F, Calandra T
pearl: In chronic pulmonary aspergillosis, echinocandins may be considered for triazole intolerance or resistance; rezafungin or ibrexafungerp are potential future alternatives.

source_paper: Pulmonary aspergillosis: diagnosis and treatment
author: Lamoth F, Calandra T
pearl: In ICU patients with severe influenza, invasive pulmonary aspergillosis incidence ranges from 5–30%; in COVID‑19 it is lower.

source_paper: Pulmonary aspergillosis: diagnosis and treatment
author: Lamoth F, Calandra T
pearl: In ICU patients, serum galactomannan sensitivity is 50–60% in influenza and <15% in COVID‑19.

source_paper: Pulmonary aspergillosis: diagnosis and treatment
author: Lamoth F, Calandra T
pearl: In ICU patients, recovery of Aspergillus from respiratory samples is a marker of poor prognosis.

source_paper: Pulmonary aspergillosis: diagnosis and treatment
author: Lamoth F, Calandra T
pearl: In ICU patients, EORTC‑MSGERC criteria do not apply; customized definitions rely on positive bronchoalveolar lavage tests.

source_paper: Pulmonary aspergillosis: diagnosis and treatment
author: Lamoth F, Calandra T
pearl: In haematologic cancer patients, serial serum galactomannan screening is not recommended during posaconazole prophylaxis due to low positive predictive value; a pre‑emptive twice‑weekly galactomannan approach is an alternative.

source_paper: Pulmonary aspergillosis: diagnosis and treatment
author: Lamoth F, Calandra T
pearl: In haematologic cancer patients, the diagnostic algorithm for IPA is: persistent febrile neutropenia or positive serum galactomannan → chest CT → bronchoscopy with BAL if a lung lesion is present.

source_paper: Pulmonary aspergillosis: diagnosis and treatment
author: Lamoth F, Calandra T
pearl: In haematologic cancer patients, culture sensitivity for Aspergillus is low (~50%); non‑culture tests such as galactomannan and PCR are the cornerstone.

source_paper: Pulmonary aspergillosis: diagnosis and treatment
author: Lamoth F, Calandra T
pearl: In haematologic cancer patients, combined bronchoalveolar lavage galactomannan and PCR yields 84–97% sensitivity and 94–97% specificity for IPA diagnosis.

source_paper: Clinical Application of Electrical Impedance Tomography in Emergency and Critical Care Medicine
author: Yoshiaki Iwashita et al.
pearl: EIT-guided PEEP titration improves lung compliance, reduces driving pressure and mechanical power compared with standard tables, but mortality benefit remains unproven and likely depends on patient phenotype.

source_paper: Clinical Application of Electrical Impedance Tomography in Emergency and Critical Care Medicine
author: Yoshiaki Iwashita et al.
pearl: EIT detects occult pendelluft and quantifies regional lung stress not captured by global ventilator signals, providing a rationale to adjust support and mitigate patient‑self‑inflicted lung injury.

source_paper: Clinical Application of Electrical Impedance Tomography in Emergency and Critical Care Medicine
author: Yoshiaki Iwashita et al.
pearl: Saline‑contrast EIT enables bedside V/Q mismatch mapping and pulmonary embolism detection; a first‑pass kinetic model allows perfusion estimation during uninterrupted breathing.

source_paper: Clinical Application of Electrical Impedance Tomography in Emergency and Critical Care Medicine
author: Yoshiaki Iwashita et al.
pearl: Hypertonic saline bolus via central venous catheter is used for EIT‑based pulmonary perfusion imaging; it requires a brief breath‑hold, which may be poorly tolerated in severe ARDS.

source_paper: Clinical Application of Electrical Impedance Tomography in Emergency and Critical Care Medicine
author: Yoshiaki Iwashita et al.
pearl: EIT can detect pneumothorax as a high‑impedance region; continuous monitoring may identify it early before overt hemodynamic deterioration.

source_paper: Clinical Application of Electrical Impedance Tomography in Emergency and Critical Care Medicine
author: Yoshiaki Iwashita et al.
pearl: EIT can detect pleural effusion through out‑of‑phase impedance changes; these changes are greater in effusion and decrease after drainage.
topic: Pleural effusion, drainage monitoring, thoracic imaging

source_paper: Clinical Application of Electrical Impedance Tomography in Emergency and Critical Care Medicine
author: Yoshiaki Iwashita et al.
pearl: EIT can identify endobronchial intubation or double‑lumen tube malposition by asymmetry in ventilation distribution.

source_paper: Clinical Application of Electrical Impedance Tomography in Emergency and Critical Care Medicine
author: Yoshiaki Iwashita et al.
pearl: EIT reveals ventilated but non‑perfused lung regions, indicating increased dead space and aiding rapid bedside recognition of pulmonary embolism.

source_paper: Clinical Application of Electrical Impedance Tomography in Emergency and Critical Care Medicine
author: Yoshiaki Iwashita et al.
pearl: EIT‑derived unmatched ventilation and perfusion are independently associated with mortality in ARDS.

source_paper: Clinical Application of Electrical Impedance Tomography in Emergency and Critical Care Medicine
author: Yoshiaki Iwashita et al.
pearl: EIT can provide continuous cardiac activity and estimate stroke volume variation; accuracy depends on lung injury type.

source_paper: Clinical Application of Electrical Impedance Tomography in Emergency and Critical Care Medicine
author: Yoshiaki Iwashita et al.
pearl: EIT can monitor gastric emptying and enteral feeding tolerance via impedance changes.
topic: Gastrointestinal monitoring, feeding tolerance, abdominal imaging

source_paper: Clinical Application of Electrical Impedance Tomography in Emergency and Critical Care Medicine
author: Yoshiaki Iwashita et al.
pearl: EIT detects early ventral‑to‑dorsal ventilation difference during spontaneous breathing trials, predicting liberation failure.

source_paper: Clinical Application of Electrical Impedance Tomography in Emergency and Critical Care Medicine
author: Yoshiaki Iwashita et al.
pearl: EIT detects pendelluft during T‑piece trials, associated with prolonged ventilation and higher 28‑day mortality.

source_paper: Clinical Application of Electrical Impedance Tomography in Emergency and Critical Care Medicine
author: Yoshiaki Iwashita et al.
pearl: EIT guides individualized ventilatory adjustments based on pendelluft magnitude and expiratory muscle activity.

source_paper: Clinical Application of Electrical Impedance Tomography in Emergency and Critical Care Medicine
author: Yoshiaki Iwashita et al.
pearl: EIT supports re‑titration of PEEP during prone positioning to maintain dorsal recruitment while avoiding ventral overdistension.

source_paper: Clinical Application of Electrical Impedance Tomography in Emergency and Critical Care Medicine
author: Yoshiaki Iwashita et al.
pearl: EIT predicts prone responsiveness and identifies non‑responders early.

source_paper: Clinical Application of Electrical Impedance Tomography in Emergency and Critical Care Medicine
author: Yoshiaki Iwashita et al.
pearl: EIT detects early deterioration during spontaneous breathing trials before overt clinical failure.

source_paper: Clinical Application of Electrical Impedance Tomography in Emergency and Critical Care Medicine
author: Yoshiaki Iwashita et al.
pearl: EIT provides bedside trend monitoring of cerebral edema; impedance changes correlate with intracranial pressure trends.

source_paper: Clinical Application of Electrical Impedance Tomography in Emergency and Critical Care Medicine
author: Yoshiaki Iwashita et al.
pearl: Brain EIT can detect acute ischemic stroke within 5 minutes of onset in an experimental rabbit model.

source_paper: Clinical Application of Electrical Impedance Tomography in Emergency and Critical Care Medicine
author: Yoshiaki Iwashita et al.
pearl: Multi‑frequency EIT may distinguish lesion types without a baseline image; robust human imaging and classification methods are still under development.

source_paper: Clinical Application of Electrical Impedance Tomography in Emergency and Critical Care Medicine
author: Yoshiaki Iwashita et al.
pearl: Non‑contact impedance‑based tomographic imaging may reduce dependence on electrode‑skin contact.

source_paper: Clinical Application of Electrical Impedance Tomography in Emergency and Critical Care Medicine
author: Yoshiaki Iwashita et al.
pearl: EIT is a soft‑field modality with limited spatial resolution; it should be viewed as complementary to CT, not a replacement.

source_paper: Clinical Application of Electrical Impedance Tomography in Emergency and Critical Care Medicine
author: Yoshiaki Iwashita et al.
pearl: Future progress depends on standardization, automation, AI‑assisted reconstruction, 3D volumetric imaging, and multimodal integration to move EIT from a research tool to routine clinical decision support.

source_paper: Nonoperative Treatment of Appendicitis and Implications for Emergency Department Management: A Narrative Review
author: David A. Talan et al.
pearl: Nonoperative treatment of uncomplicated appendicitis with antibiotics achieves a 90 % initial response rate and allows about two‑thirds of patients to avoid surgery at 1 year, with faster pain resolution and less disability compared to appendectomy.

source_paper: Nonoperative Treatment of Appendicitis and Implications for Emergency Department Management: A Narrative Review
author: David A. Talan et al.
pearl: The presence of an appendicolith is associated with a 52 % 1‑year NOTA failure rate and higher complication rates, making it a relative contraindication to nonoperative management.

source_paper: Nonoperative Treatment of Appendicitis and Implications for Emergency Department Management: A Narrative Review
author: David A. Talan et al.
pearl: Emergency department discharge and outpatient management of NOTA patients is feasible in up to 90 % of adults, with no significant increase in serious adverse events or return visits compared to hospitalization.

source_paper: Nonoperative Treatment of Appendicitis and Implications for Emergency Department Management: A Narrative Review
author: David A. Talan et al.
pearl: The risk of missing an appendiceal cancer with NOTA is very low (0.5 % over 2 years), all detected cases being stage 1 with no significant pre‑diagnosis progression.

source_paper: Nonoperative Treatment of Appendicitis and Implications for Emergency Department Management: A Narrative Review
author: David A. Talan et al.
pearl: Shared decision‑making is optimal; most informed patients would choose NOTA even if the 1‑year appendectomy rate were 50 %.

source_paper: Nonoperative Treatment of Appendicitis and Implications for Emergency Department Management: A Narrative Review
author: David A. Talan et al.
pearl: Cost‑effectiveness analyses show that increasing NOTA use from 20 % to 50 % in the US could save approximately $192 million annually in societal costs.

source_paper: Nonoperative Treatment of Appendicitis and Implications for Emergency Department Management: A Narrative Review
author: David A. Talan et al.
pearl: No deaths have been reported among more than 4,000 children and adults treated with either NOTA or surgery in the major comparative trials.

source_paper: Nonoperative Treatment of Appendicitis and Implications for Emergency Department Management: A Narrative Review
author: David A. Talan et al.
pearl: In the CODA trial, 46 % of NOTA adults were discharged from the ED; discharge criteria included stability after long‑acting IV antibiotics, tolerating oral intake, and controlled pain.

source_paper: Nonoperative Treatment of Appendicitis and Implications for Emergency Department Management: A Narrative Review
author: David A. Talan et al.
pearl: Outpatient NOTA management is safe, with 7‑day serious adverse events of 0.9 per 100 outpatients versus 1.3 per 100 inpatients, and is associated with fewer appendectomies and one day less disability.

source_paper: Nonoperative Treatment of Appendicitis and Implications for Emergency Department Management: A Narrative Review
author: David A. Talan et al.
pearl: Laparoscopic appendectomy complications per 1,000 procedures include wound infection 38, abscess 20, and bowel obstruction 3.

source_paper: Nonoperative Treatment of Appendicitis and Implications for Emergency Department Management: A Narrative Review
author: David A. Talan et al.
pearl: The 30‑day case‑fatality rate for nonperforated appendicitis surgery is 0.5 per 1,000.

source_paper: Nonoperative Treatment of Appendicitis and Implications for Emergency Department Management: A Narrative Review
author: David A. Talan et al.
pearl: Initial antibiotic regimens for NOTA include parenteral ertapenem, piperacillin‑tazobactam, or ceftriaxone plus metronidazole, followed by oral therapy to complete 7–10 days.

source_paper: Nonoperative Treatment of Appendicitis and Implications for Emergency Department Management: A Narrative Review
author: David A. Talan et al.
pearl: Appendectomy failure rates decline rapidly; more than 50 % of NOTA failures occur within the first month.

source_paper: Nonoperative Treatment of Appendicitis and Implications for Emergency Department Management: A Narrative Review
author: David A. Talan et al.
pearl: The 5‑year cumulative appendectomy rates after NOTA range from 27 % to 39 %.

source_paper: Nonoperative Treatment of Appendicitis and Implications for Emergency Department Management: A Narrative Review
author: David A. Talan et al.
pearl: In the CODA non‑appendicolith subgroup, 1‑ and 2‑year NOTA appendectomy rates were 36 % and 42 %.

source_paper: Nonoperative Treatment of Appendicitis and Implications for Emergency Department Management: A Narrative Review
author: David A. Talan et al.
pearl: Serious adverse event rates are similar between NOTA and surgery, at about 3 %.

source_paper: Nonoperative Treatment of Appendicitis and Implications for Emergency Department Management: A Narrative Review
author: David A. Talan et al.
pearl: The true efficacy of NOTA may be higher than reported due to trial design biases favoring surgery.

source_paper: Nonoperative Treatment of Appendicitis and Implications for Emergency Department Management: A Narrative Review
author: David A. Talan et al.
pearl: Emergency physicians can expand NOTA access through shared decision‑making and outpatient management, potentially via telemedicine in rural areas.

source_paper: Nonoperative Treatment of Appendicitis and Implications for Emergency Department Management: A Narrative Review
author: David A. Talan et al.
pearl: In disadvantaged populations, NOTA reduces lost work days (14 vs 7 for surgery vs NOTA in CODA Spanish‑speaking cohort).

source_paper: Nonoperative Treatment of Appendicitis and Implications for Emergency Department Management: A Narrative Review
author: David A. Talan et al.
pearl: US national data 2016‑2020 show only 11.7 % of adults with uncomplicated appendicitis received NOTA.
